lessons.title: Your first commands
lessons.description: Understand what the terminal is and run your first 4 commands

steps.label: What is the terminal?
steps.text:
The <hl>terminal</hl> is a text interface where you type commands to control your computer. Unlike clicking icons, you type exactly what you want Linux to do.

Every command follows the same pattern:
<cmd>command [options] [arguments]</cmd>

When you open a terminal you'll see a <hl>prompt</hl> ending in <hl>$</hl> — that means Linux is waiting for your input.

steps.label: Quick check
steps.question: What does the $ symbol at the end of a prompt mean?
options: You are logged in as root (admin)
The terminal is ready for your command

feedback.ok: Correct! $ means the shell is ready and waiting for your input.
feedback.bad: Not quite — $ simply signals that the shell is idle and ready for a new command.

steps.label: Fill in the blank
sentence: To print a message to the terminal, use:
"Good morning"

feedback.ok: Correct! echo prints any text you give it straight to the terminal.
feedback.bad: Not quite — echo is the command for printing text. print and say don't exist as standard commands.

steps.label: Which command?
steps.question: You want to know which user account you're currently logged in as. Which command do you run?

feedback.ok: Correct! whoami prints your current username.
feedback.bad: whoami is the command that prints your current logged-in username.

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: Print the text 'I love Linux' to the terminal.
steps.hint: Use echo followed by the text you want to print.

output.bad:
<error>✗ Not quite.</error>
<info>Hint: echo "your text here" prints text to the terminal.</info>

lessons.title: Where am I? — pwd
lessons.description: Find your current location in the Linux filesystem

steps.label: The filesystem tree
steps.text:
Linux organises everything in a tree starting at <hl>/</hl> (called the <hl>root</hl>). Every file and folder has an address called a <hl>path</hl>.

<cmd>pwd</cmd> stands for <hl>print working directory</hl>. It tells you exactly where you are in the tree right now.

Your home folder is always at <hl>/home/your-username</hl> and is also written as <hl>~</hl> (tilde).

steps.label: Fill in the blank
sentence: To find out your current directory location, type:

feedback.ok: Correct! pwd = print working directory.
feedback.bad: Not quite — pwd (print working directory) shows your current location.

steps.label: Reading a path
steps.question: pwd outputs /home/user/projects. What does this tell you?
options: You are in the root / directory
You are in a folder called projects, inside user, inside home
You are in your home folder ~

feedback.ok: Correct! Paths are read left to right — / is root, then home, then user, then projects.
feedback.bad: Paths read left to right. /home/user/projects means you're inside projects, which is inside user, which is inside home.

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: Print your current working directory to the terminal.
steps.hint: Three letters: print working directory.

output.bad:
<error>✗ Not quite.</error>
<info>Hint: pwd stands for print working directory.</info>

lessons.title: Listing files — ls
lessons.description: See what's inside a directory, and use flags for more detail

steps.label: The ls command
steps.text:
<cmd>ls</cmd> lists the files and folders in your current directory.

Useful flags:
<cmd>ls -l</cmd> — long format (size, date, permissions)
<cmd>ls -a</cmd> — show hidden files (names starting with .)
<cmd>ls -lh</cmd> — long format with human-readable sizes (KB, MB)

Flags can be combined: <cmd>ls -la</cmd> shows hidden files in long format.

terminal.output:
drwxr-xr-x 2 user user 4096 Mar 23 Documents
drwxr-xr-x 2 user user 4096 Mar 23 Downloads
-rw-r--r-- 1 user user  128 Mar 23 notes.txt
-rwxr-xr-x 1 user user  512 Mar 23 script.sh

steps.label: Quick check
steps.question: Which flag makes ls show hidden files (files starting with a dot)?

feedback.ok: Correct! -a shows all files including hidden ones.
feedback.bad: The -a flag stands for 'all' — it reveals hidden files whose names start with a dot.

steps.label: Fill in the blank
sentence: To list files in long format with human-readable sizes, type:
-lh

feedback.ok: Correct! ls -lh gives you file details with sizes in KB/MB instead of raw bytes.
feedback.bad: ls is the listing command. ls -lh adds long format and human-readable sizes.

steps.label: Flag practice
sentence: To see hidden files in long format, combine flags: ls

feedback.ok: Correct! -la combines -l (long) and -a (all/hidden) into one flag.
feedback.bad: -la combines -l for long format and -a for all files including hidden.

steps.label: Reorder the command
steps.instruction: Arrange these tokens to list all files including hidden ones in long format:

feedback.ok: Correct! Command first, flags second, path last.
feedback.bad: Order matters: command (ls) → flags (-la) → target path (/home/user).

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: List all files in the current directory including hidden files.
steps.hint: Use the -a flag to show hidden files.

output.bad:
<error>✗ Not quite.</error>
<info>Hint: add the -a flag to ls to show hidden files.</info>

lessons.title: Changing directories — cd
lessons.description: Move around the filesystem using absolute and relative paths

steps.label: The cd command
steps.text:
<cmd>cd</cmd> stands for <hl>change directory</hl>. It moves you to a different location in the filesystem.

Useful shortcuts:
<cmd>cd ~</cmd> — go to your home directory
<cmd>cd ..</cmd> — go up one level (parent directory)
<cmd>cd -</cmd> — go back to the previous directory
<cmd>cd /</cmd> — go to the root directory

<hl>Absolute path</hl>: starts from root — <cmd>/home/user/Documents</cmd>
<hl>Relative path</hl>: from where you are now — <cmd>Documents/projects</cmd>

steps.label: Quick check
steps.question: You are in /home/user/projects/app. Which command takes you up one level to /home/user/projects?

feedback.ok: Correct! cd .. always moves you one level up to the parent directory.
feedback.bad: cd .. moves up one directory level. ~ goes to home, / goes to root.

steps.label: Fill in the blank
sentence: To return to your home directory from anywhere, type:

feedback.ok: Correct! cd ~ is the fastest way home from anywhere.
feedback.bad: cd is the change directory command. cd ~ takes you home.

steps.label: Absolute vs relative
sentence: An absolute path always starts with
(the root)

feedback.ok: Correct! Absolute paths always start from / (root), like /home/user/Documents.
feedback.bad: Absolute paths start from / (root). Relative paths start from your current location.

steps.label: Reorder the command
steps.instruction: Arrange these to navigate to the Documents folder inside your home directory:

feedback.ok: Correct! cd ~/Documents takes you directly to Documents in your home folder.
feedback.bad: cd comes first, then the path: cd ~/Documents

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: Navigate to the /etc directory using an absolute path.
steps.hint: Use cd with an absolute path starting from /

output.bad:
<error>✗ Not quite.</error>
<info>Hint: cd /etc — use the full absolute path starting with /</info>

lessons.title: Creating directories — mkdir
lessons.description: Create new directories, including nested ones with -p

steps.label: Making directories
steps.text:
<cmd>mkdir</cmd> stands for <hl>make directory</hl>. It creates a new folder.

<cmd>mkdir projects</cmd> — creates a folder called projects here
<cmd>mkdir -p a/b/c</cmd> — creates nested folders in one go (the <hl>-p</hl> flag creates parent directories as needed)
<cmd>mkdir dir1 dir2 dir3</cmd> — create multiple folders at once

steps.label: Fill in the blank
sentence: To create nested folders in one command, use: mkdir
parent/child/grandchild

feedback.ok: Correct! -p creates all parent directories that don't exist yet.
feedback.bad: -p tells mkdir to create all parent directories along the path.

steps.label: Quick check
steps.question: You run mkdir a/b/c but folder 'a' doesn't exist yet. What happens?
options: Linux creates a, b, and c automatically
Only c is created inside the current directory
You get an error: no such file or directory
Linux asks you to confirm each folder

feedback.ok: Correct! Without -p, mkdir fails if the parent doesn't exist. Use mkdir -p to avoid this.
feedback.bad: Without -p, mkdir fails if any parent directory is missing. Always use mkdir -p for nested paths.

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: Create a nested folder structure: projects/linux/section1 in one command.
steps.hint: Use mkdir -p to create all parent folders at once.

output.ok: <success>✓ Correct! Three nested directories created in one go.</success>
output.bad:
<error>✗ Not quite.</error>
<info>Hint: mkdir -p projects/linux/section1 creates all folders along the path.</info>

lessons.title: Creating, copying & moving files
lessons.description: Create empty files, copy them, and move or rename them

steps.label: touch, cp, mv
steps.text:
<cmd>touch filename</cmd> — creates an empty file (or updates its timestamp if it exists)

<cmd>cp source destination</cmd> — copies a file
<cmd>cp -r folder/ destination/</cmd> — copy an entire folder (-r means recursive)

<cmd>mv source destination</cmd> — moves a file to a new location
<cmd>mv oldname newname</cmd> — renames a file (mv is also how you rename!)

steps.label: Fill in the blank
sentence: To rename a file from old.txt to new.txt:
old.txt new.txt

feedback.ok: Correct! mv is used for both moving AND renaming files.
feedback.bad: mv (move) is used for renaming too. mv old.txt new.txt renames the file in place.

steps.label: Copying folders
sentence: To copy an entire directory, use: cp
myfolder/ backup/

feedback.ok: Correct! -r means recursive — it copies the folder and everything inside it.
feedback.bad: -r (recursive) is needed to copy a folder and all its contents.

steps.label: Quick check
steps.question: What does touch do if the file already exists?
options: It deletes and recreates the file
It updates the file's last-modified timestamp

feedback.ok: Correct! touch on an existing file just updates its timestamp without changing its content.
feedback.bad: touch on an existing file updates its timestamp. It's a safe command — it never overwrites content.

steps.label: Reorder the command
steps.instruction: Arrange these tokens to copy report.txt into the Documents folder:

feedback.ok: Correct! cp source destination — source first, destination second.
feedback.bad: cp takes source first, then destination: cp report.txt Documents/

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: Create an empty file called index.html in the current directory.
steps.hint: Use touch to create an empty file.

output.bad:
<error>✗ Not quite.</error>
<info>Hint: touch index.html creates an empty file.</info>

lessons.title: Deleting files & directories — rm
lessons.description: Delete files and empty directories safely — no recycle bin!

steps.label: rm and rmdir
steps.text:
<cmd>rm filename</cmd> — deletes a file permanently
<cmd>rm -r folder/</cmd> — deletes a folder and everything inside it
<cmd>rm -i filename</cmd> — asks for confirmation before deleting (safe mode)
<cmd>rmdir folder/</cmd> — deletes an empty directory only

<warn>Warning: Linux has NO recycle bin. rm is permanent. Always double-check before running rm -r.</warn>

steps.label: Safety check
steps.question: You run rm -r /home/user/documents. What happens to the documents folder?
options: It moves to the Recycle Bin
It is permanently deleted along with everything inside
Linux asks for confirmation for each file

feedback.ok: Correct! rm -r is permanent and immediate. There is no undo.
feedback.bad: rm -r permanently deletes the folder and all its contents. There is no recycle bin in Linux.

steps.label: Which command?
steps.question: You want to delete a folder but only if it's empty. Which command should you use?

feedback.ok: Correct! rmdir only works on empty directories — it's a safer option.
feedback.bad: rmdir only deletes empty directories. It will fail with an error if the folder has any contents — making it safer than rm -r.

steps.label: Safe deletion
sentence: To delete with confirmation prompts, use: rm
filename

feedback.ok: Correct! -i (interactive) makes rm ask before deleting each file.
feedback.bad: -i makes rm ask for confirmation before each deletion. Good habit when deleting important files.

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: Delete a file called temp.log from the current directory.
steps.hint: Use rm followed by the filename.

output.bad:
<error>✗ Not quite.</error>
<info>Hint: rm temp.log deletes the file permanently.</info>

lessons.title: Section 1 review
lessons.description: Reinforce everything from section 1 with mixed exercises

steps.label: Review — navigation
steps.question: Which command shows you your current directory location?

feedback.ok: Correct! pwd = print working directory.
feedback.bad: pwd (print working directory) shows where you are.

steps.label: Review — fill blank
sentence: To go up one directory level: cd

feedback.ok: Correct! cd .. moves to the parent directory.
feedback.bad: cd .. (two dots) moves you up one level to the parent directory.

steps.label: Review — reorder
steps.instruction: Arrange to create a new directory called myapp:

feedback.ok: Correct! mkdir myapp creates the directory.
feedback.bad: Command first: mkdir myapp

steps.label: Review — files
steps.question: Which command creates an empty file called notes.txt?

feedback.ok: Correct! touch creates a new empty file.
feedback.bad: touch creates empty files. mkdir creates directories.

steps.label: Review — copying
sentence: To copy file.txt to backup.txt:
file.txt backup.txt

feedback.ok: Correct! cp copies, mv moves/renames.
feedback.bad: cp (copy) duplicates the file. mv would move/rename it instead.

steps.label: Review — write it
steps.prompt: Move the file report.txt into the Documents folder.
steps.hint: mv source destination

output.bad:
<error>✗ Not quite.</error>
<info>Hint: mv report.txt Documents/ — source then destination.</info>

steps.label: Review — write it
steps.prompt: List all files including hidden ones in long format.
steps.hint: Combine -l and -a flags.

output.ok:
<success>✓ Correct!</success>
<info>total 48
drwxr-xr-x  8 user user 4096 Mar 23 .
drwxr-xr-x 12 root root 4096 Mar 23 ..
-rw-r--r--  1 user user  220 Mar 23 .bashrc</info>
output.bad:
<error>✗ Not quite.</error>
<info>Hint: ls -la shows all files including hidden ones in long format.</info>

sections.title: Reading & writing files
sections.subtitle: View, search, and edit file content

lessons.title: Printing file contents — cat
lessons.description: Display file contents and concatenate multiple files

steps.label: The cat command
steps.text:
<cmd>cat</cmd> stands for <hl>concatenate</hl>. It reads files and prints their contents to the terminal.

<cmd>cat file.txt</cmd> — print entire file
<cmd>cat file1.txt file2.txt</cmd> — print both files one after another
<cmd>cat -n file.txt</cmd> — show line numbers

Best for small files. For large files, use <cmd>less</cmd> instead — cat will flood your screen.

terminal.output:
Meeting at 10am
Buy groceries
Finish Linux course

1	Meeting at 10am
     2	Buy groceries
     3	Finish Linux course

steps.label: Fill in the blank
sentence: To print file contents with line numbers: cat
file.txt

feedback.ok: Correct! cat -n prefixes every line with its line number.
feedback.bad: -n adds line numbers to the output.

steps.label: When to use cat
steps.question: You need to read a 50,000-line server log file. Should you use cat?
options: Yes, cat is always the right choice for files
No — use less instead so you can scroll through it
Yes but with cat -n for line numbers
No — use ls to read files

feedback.ok: Correct! cat dumps everything at once — use less for large files so you can scroll.
feedback.bad: cat prints everything at once, flooding your terminal. For large files, less lets you scroll page by page.

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: Print the contents of a file called config.txt to the terminal.
steps.hint: cat followed by the filename.

output.bad:
<error>✗ Not quite.</error>
<info>Hint: cat config.txt prints the file's contents.</info>

lessons.title: First & last lines — head, tail
lessons.description: Preview the top or bottom of a file; follow live logs with tail -f

steps.label: head and tail
steps.text:
<cmd>head file.txt</cmd> — shows the first 10 lines (default)
<cmd>head -n 5 file.txt</cmd> — shows only the first 5 lines

<cmd>tail file.txt</cmd> — shows the last 10 lines
<cmd>tail -n 3 file.txt</cmd> — shows the last 3 lines
<cmd>tail -f logfile</cmd> — <hl>follows</hl> the file in real time (great for watching logs live). Press Ctrl+C to stop.

terminal.output:
[INFO] Server started
[INFO] Listening on port 8080
[INFO] Database connected

[INFO] New request from 192.168.1.1
[INFO] Response sent in 12ms
(following...)

steps.label: Fill in the blank
sentence: To see the first 5 lines of a file:
-n 5 file.txt

feedback.ok: Correct! head shows lines from the top of the file.
feedback.bad: head reads from the top of the file. tail reads from the bottom.

steps.label: Live log watching
sentence: To watch a log file update in real time: tail
app.log

feedback.ok: Correct! -f means follow — tail -f stays open and prints new lines as they're written.
feedback.bad: -f makes tail follow the file, printing new lines as they arrive. Essential for watching live logs.

steps.label: head or tail?
steps.question: You want to see the most recent 10 entries in a log file. Which command is most useful?

feedback.ok: Correct! tail -n 10 shows the last 10 lines — the most recent entries.
feedback.bad: tail reads from the bottom (most recent). tail -n 10 gives you the last 10 lines.

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: Show the last 20 lines of a file called error.log
steps.hint: Use tail with -n to specify the number of lines.

output.bad:
<error>✗ Not quite.</error>
<info>Hint: tail -n 20 error.log shows the last 20 lines.</info>

lessons.title: Scrolling through files — less
lessons.description: Read large files page by page with keyboard navigation

steps.label: The less command
steps.text:
<cmd>less file.txt</cmd> opens the file in a scrollable viewer — it doesn't dump everything at once.

Keyboard controls inside less:
<cmd>Space</cmd> or <cmd>f</cmd> — next page
<cmd>b</cmd> — previous page
<cmd>g</cmd> — go to beginning
<cmd>G</cmd> — go to end
<cmd>/searchterm</cmd> — search forward
<cmd>n</cmd> — jump to next search match
<cmd>q</cmd> — quit

less is smarter than more — it lets you scroll both ways and search.

terminal.output:
(opens file in scrollable viewer)
(press q to quit, /term to search)

steps.label: Keyboard shortcut
steps.question: You are inside less reading a file. How do you quit?

feedback.ok: Correct! q quits less and returns you to the terminal.
feedback.bad: Press q to quit less. This is the standard quit key in most Linux file viewers.

steps.label: Searching in less
steps.question: You're reading a file in less and want to find the word 'error'. What do you type?

feedback.ok: Correct! /searchterm searches forward in less. Press n to jump to the next match.
feedback.bad: Inside less, type /error to search. Press n to go to the next match.

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: Open the file server.log in the less viewer.
steps.hint: less followed by the filename.

output.ok: <success>✓ Correct! server.log opens in less. (Press q to quit)</success>
output.bad:
<error>✗ Not quite.</error>
<info>Hint: less server.log opens the file in a scrollable viewer.</info>

lessons.title: Counting content — wc
lessons.description: Count lines, words, and characters in a file

steps.label: The wc command
steps.text:
<cmd>wc</cmd> stands for <hl>word count</hl>. It counts lines, words, and bytes in a file.

<cmd>wc file.txt</cmd> — shows lines, words, bytes
<cmd>wc -l file.txt</cmd> — count lines only
<cmd>wc -w file.txt</cmd> — count words only
<cmd>wc -c file.txt</cmd> — count bytes/characters

Very useful in pipelines: <cmd>ls | wc -l</cmd> counts how many files are in a directory.

steps.label: Fill in the blank
sentence: To count only the number of lines in a file: wc
file.txt

feedback.ok: Correct! wc -l counts lines. -w counts words, -c counts characters.
feedback.bad: -l counts lines. Remember: l=lines, w=words, c=characters.

steps.label: Reading wc output
steps.question: wc report.txt outputs: 42  318  1840 report.txt. What does 42 represent?

feedback.ok: Correct! wc output order is: lines, words, bytes (characters).
feedback.bad: wc output is always: lines words bytes. So 42 = lines, 318 = words, 1840 = bytes.

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: Count how many lines are in a file called data.csv
steps.hint: Use wc with the -l flag for line count.

output.bad:
<error>✗ Not quite.</error>
<info>Hint: wc -l data.csv counts the number of lines.</info>

lessons.title: Editing files — nano
lessons.description: Create and edit text files directly in the terminal

steps.label: The nano editor
steps.text:
<cmd>nano filename</cmd> opens a beginner-friendly text editor right in the terminal.

Key shortcuts (shown at the bottom of nano):
<cmd>Ctrl+O</cmd> — save (write Out)
<cmd>Ctrl+X</cmd> — exit
<cmd>Ctrl+W</cmd> — search (Where is)
<cmd>Ctrl+K</cmd> — cut a line
<cmd>Ctrl+U</cmd> — paste

The ^ symbol in nano means Ctrl. So ^X = Ctrl+X.

terminal.output:
(nano opens — you can type freely)
(Ctrl+O to save, Ctrl+X to exit)

steps.label: Nano shortcuts
steps.question: In nano, what does Ctrl+O do?

feedback.ok: Correct! Ctrl+O saves (writes Out) the current file.
feedback.bad: Ctrl+O saves the file. Ctrl+X exits. Ctrl+W searches. Ctrl+O = write Out.

steps.label: The ^ symbol
steps.question: Nano shows ^X at the bottom. What key combination does this mean?

feedback.ok: Correct! In Linux documentation, ^ always means Ctrl.
feedback.bad: In Linux, ^ = Ctrl. So ^X = Ctrl+X, which exits nano.

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: Open a file called shopping.txt in the nano editor.
steps.hint: nano followed by the filename.

output.ok: <success>✓ Correct! nano shopping.txt opens the file for editing.</success>
output.bad:
<error>✗ Not quite.</error>
<info>Hint: nano shopping.txt opens or creates the file in the nano editor.</info>

lessons.title: Writing to files — redirection
lessons.description: Save command output to files; overwrite vs append

steps.label: Output redirection
steps.text:
By default, commands print to the terminal. You can <hl>redirect</hl> output into a file instead.

<cmd>command > file.txt</cmd> — writes output to file (<hl>overwrites</hl> if it exists)
<cmd>command >> file.txt</cmd> — <hl>appends</hl> output to file (adds to the end)

Examples:
<cmd>echo "hello" > greet.txt</cmd> — creates greet.txt with 'hello'
<cmd>ls >> inventory.txt</cmd> — appends directory listing to inventory.txt
<cmd>date >> log.txt</cmd> — logs current date to a file

steps.label: Overwrite or append?
sentence: To ADD output to the end of a file without erasing it, use: echo 'text'
file.txt

feedback.ok: Correct! >> appends. > overwrites. Easy to remember: >> is like 'adding more'.
feedback.bad: >> appends to the end. > overwrites. Always double-check which one you mean!

steps.label: Save command output
sentence: Save the output of ls into a file called filelist.txt: ls
filelist.txt

feedback.ok: Correct! > redirects command output into a file.
feedback.bad: > redirects (overwrites). Use >> if you want to append instead.

steps.label: Overwrite warning
steps.question: You have a file secrets.txt with important data. You accidentally run: echo 'oops' > secrets.txt. What happened?
options: secrets.txt now contains 'oops' — original data is gone
An error is shown — you can't overwrite existing files
secrets.txt now has 'oops' appended to the end
Linux creates a backup before overwriting

feedback.ok: Correct! > overwrites without warning. The original content is gone permanently.
feedback.bad: > silently overwrites. There is no warning or backup. This is a common and painful mistake.

steps.label: Reorder the command
steps.instruction: Arrange these to append today's date to a file called diary.txt:

feedback.ok: Correct! Command first, then redirection operator, then filename.
feedback.bad: Order: command >> filename — date >> diary.txt

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: Write the text 'LinuxPath progress' into a new file called progress.txt
steps.hint: Use echo with > to redirect into a file.

output.ok: <success>✓ Correct! progress.txt created with your text.</success>
output.bad:
<error>✗ Not quite.</error>
<info>Hint: echo 'your text' > filename.txt writes text to a file.</info>

lessons.title: Section 2 review
lessons.description: Reinforce reading and writing commands with mixed exercises

steps.label: Review — cat vs less
steps.question: Which command is better for reading a 10,000-line file?

feedback.ok: Correct! less lets you scroll without flooding the terminal.
feedback.bad: less is better for large files — it shows one page at a time and lets you scroll.

steps.label: Review — tail follow
sentence: To watch a log file update live in real time: tail
app.log

feedback.ok: Correct! tail -f follows the file and shows new lines as they're written.
feedback.bad: -f makes tail follow the file in real time.

steps.label: Review — word count
sentence: To count the number of words in essay.txt: wc
essay.txt

feedback.ok: Correct! wc -w counts words.
feedback.bad: wc -w counts words. -l counts lines. -c counts characters.

steps.label: Review — reorder
steps.instruction: Arrange to save the output of date into a file called now.txt:

feedback.ok: Correct! date > now.txt saves the current date into now.txt.
feedback.bad: Command > filename: date > now.txt

steps.label: Review — append
steps.question: What is the difference between > and >> in redirection?
options: > is for text files, >> is for binary files

feedback.ok: Correct! > overwrites, >> appends. Never mix these up on important files!
feedback.bad: > overwrites the file (danger!). >> appends to the end (safe). Always double-check.

steps.label: Review — write it
steps.prompt: Show the last 5 lines of a file called access.log
steps.hint: tail with -n flag.

output.bad:
<error>✗ Not quite.</error>
<info>Hint: tail -n 5 access.log shows the last 5 lines.</info>

steps.label: Review — write it
steps.prompt: Print the first 3 lines of a file called config.yml
steps.hint: head with -n flag.

output.bad:
<error>✗ Not quite.</error>
<info>Hint: head -n 3 config.yml shows the first 3 lines.</info>

lessons.title: Searching text — grep
lessons.description: Search for patterns inside files with powerful flags

steps.label: The grep command
steps.text:
<cmd>grep</cmd> searches for text patterns inside files. It prints every line that matches.

<cmd>grep 'word' file.txt</cmd> — find lines containing 'word'
<cmd>grep -i 'word' file.txt</cmd> — case-insensitive search
<cmd>grep -r 'word' folder/</cmd> — search recursively through all files in a folder
<cmd>grep -n 'word' file.txt</cmd> — show line numbers
<cmd>grep -v 'word' file.txt</cmd> — show lines that do NOT match (invert)
<cmd>grep -c 'word' file.txt</cmd> — count matching lines

terminal.output:
[ERROR] Connection refused at 09:14
[ERROR] Timeout after 30s at 09:22

steps.label: Case-insensitive search
sentence: To search for 'warning' ignoring uppercase/lowercase: grep
'warning' file.txt

feedback.ok: Correct! -i makes grep ignore case — it matches WARNING, Warning, warning etc.
feedback.bad: -i means case-insensitive. It will match 'warning', 'WARNING', 'Warning' all at once.

steps.label: Recursive search
sentence: To search for 'TODO' in all files inside a folder: grep
'TODO' src/

feedback.ok: Correct! -r searches recursively through every file in the folder.
feedback.bad: -r (recursive) tells grep to search through all files inside the folder and its subfolders.

steps.label: Invert match
steps.question: You want to see all lines in a file that do NOT contain the word 'debug'. Which flag do you use?

feedback.ok: Correct! -v inverts the match — shows lines that don't contain the pattern.
feedback.bad: -v inverts grep: it shows every line that does NOT match the pattern.

steps.label: Reorder the command
steps.instruction: Arrange to recursively search for 'password' in all files in the config/ folder:

feedback.ok: Correct! grep -r 'pattern' folder/ searches all files recursively.
feedback.bad: grep goes first, then flags, then pattern in quotes, then the folder: grep -r 'password' config/

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: Search for the word 'failed' (case-insensitive) with line numbers in auth.log
steps.hint: Combine -i (case-insensitive) and -n (line numbers) flags.

output.ok:
<success>✓ Correct! Searching case-insensitively with line numbers.</success>
<info>12:Failed password for user admin
47:FAILED login attempt from 10.0.0.5</info>
output.bad:
<error>✗ Not quite.</error>
<info>Hint: grep -in 'failed' auth.log combines -i and -n flags.</info>

lessons.title: Finding files — find
lessons.description: Locate files and directories by name, type, size, and date

steps.label: The find command
steps.text:
<cmd>find</cmd> searches the filesystem for files matching criteria.

<cmd>find . -name 'file.txt'</cmd> — find by exact name in current dir
<cmd>find . -name '*.log'</cmd> — find all .log files (wildcards work)
<cmd>find . -type d</cmd> — find directories only (-type f for files)
<cmd>find . -size +1M</cmd> — find files larger than 1MB
<cmd>find . -mtime -7</cmd> — find files modified in the last 7 days

The first argument is <hl>where to search</hl>. Use <hl>.</hl> for current directory.

steps.label: Find by type
sentence: To find only directories (not files) under /home: find /home -type

feedback.ok: Correct! -type d finds directories, -type f finds regular files, -type l finds symlinks.
feedback.bad: -type d finds directories. -type f finds regular files.

steps.label: Find by name pattern
sentence: To find all Python files recursively from current dir: find . -name

feedback.ok: Correct! *.py uses a wildcard to match any file ending in .py.
feedback.bad: Use '*.py' with quotes and a wildcard — quotes prevent the shell from expanding * too early.

steps.label: Find recently modified
steps.question: You want to find files modified within the last 3 days. Which option do you use?

feedback.ok: Correct! -mtime -3 means 'modified time less than 3 days ago'.
feedback.bad: -mtime -3 finds files modified within the last 3 days. Negative means within, positive means older than.

feedback.ok: Correct! find [where] [criteria] — path comes before the -name flag.
feedback.bad: find needs: find [search location] [options]. So: find /var/log -name '*.log'

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: Find all files larger than 100MB in the current directory.
steps.hint: Use find with -size and the + prefix for 'larger than'.

output.bad:
<error>✗ Not quite.</error>
<info>Hint: find . -size +100M finds files over 100 megabytes.</info>

lessons.title: Finding programs — which, whereis
lessons.description: Quickly find where commands and programs are installed

steps.label: Locating commands
steps.text:
<cmd>which command</cmd> — shows the full path of a command you'd run
<cmd>whereis command</cmd> — finds the binary, source, and manual page locations
<cmd>locate filename</cmd> — fast filename search using a pre-built database (run <cmd>sudo updatedb</cmd> to refresh)

Quick comparison:
<hl>which</hl> — where is the executable I'd run?
<hl>whereis</hl> — where is everything related to this program?
<hl>locate</hl> — where is any file by name? (fastest, but can be outdated)

steps.label: which vs whereis
steps.question: You want to know exactly which version of Python will run when you type python3. Which command tells you this?

feedback.ok: Correct! which shows the exact executable path that would run.
feedback.bad: which shows the executable that would run when you type the command. It's the fastest way to check which version is active.

steps.label: locate speed
steps.question: Why is locate faster than find for searching files by name?
options: locate searches a pre-built index database, not live filesystem
locate only searches your home folder

feedback.ok: Correct! locate reads from a database (updatedb builds it). find scans the actual filesystem in real time.
feedback.bad: locate queries a pre-built index database, which is much faster than find scanning every file live.

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: Find out where the 'git' executable is located on your system.
steps.hint: Use which to find where a command lives.

output.bad:
<error>✗ Not quite.</error>
<info>Hint: which git shows the path of the git executable.</info>

lessons.title: Sorting & deduplication — sort, uniq
lessons.description: Sort lines alphabetically or numerically; remove or count duplicates

steps.label: sort and uniq
steps.text:
<cmd>sort file.txt</cmd> — sort lines alphabetically
<cmd>sort -n file.txt</cmd> — sort numerically
<cmd>sort -r file.txt</cmd> — reverse sort
<cmd>sort -u file.txt</cmd> — sort and remove duplicates

<cmd>uniq file.txt</cmd> — remove consecutive duplicate lines (file must be sorted first)
<cmd>uniq -c file.txt</cmd> — count how many times each line appears
<cmd>uniq -d file.txt</cmd> — show only duplicate lines

Classic combo: <cmd>sort file.txt | uniq -c | sort -rn</cmd>

steps.label: Numeric sort
sentence: To sort a file by number instead of alphabetically: sort
numbers.txt

feedback.ok: Correct! Without -n, sort would order 10 before 2 (alphabetically). -n sorts properly by value.
feedback.bad: -n sorts numerically. Without it, 10 comes before 2 alphabetically which is rarely what you want.

steps.label: uniq -c
steps.question: What does sort names.txt | uniq -c output?
options: Names sorted alphabetically with no duplicates
Each unique name with a count of how many times it appears
Only names that appear more than once

feedback.ok: Correct! uniq -c prefixes each line with the count of consecutive occurrences.
feedback.bad: uniq -c counts how many times each line appears. Combined with sort first, it's a frequency counter.

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: Sort the file cities.txt and remove duplicate entries.
steps.hint: Either sort -u or pipe sort into uniq.

output.ok:
<success>✓ Correct! Sorted with duplicates removed.</success>
<info>Amsterdam
Berlin
Chennai
Delhi
Paris</info>
output.bad:
<error>✗ Not quite.</error>
<info>Hint: sort -u cities.txt sorts and removes duplicates in one go.</info>

lessons.title: Chaining commands — pipes
lessons.description: Connect commands together so the output of one feeds into the next

steps.label: The pipe operator
steps.text:
The <hl>pipe</hl> <cmd>|</cmd> connects commands — the output of the left command becomes the input of the right command.

This is the Unix philosophy: small tools, chained together.

<cmd>ls | wc -l</cmd> — count files in current directory
<cmd>cat file.txt | grep 'error'</cmd> — find errors in a file
<cmd>ps aux | grep 'python'</cmd> — find running python processes
<cmd>cat access.log | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn</cmd> — count unique visitors

You can chain as many commands as you need.

terminal.cmd: cat access.log | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -5

steps.label: Count with pipes
sentence: To count how many files are in the current directory: ls
wc -l

feedback.ok: Correct! | pipes ls output into wc -l which counts the lines.
feedback.bad: | (pipe) sends the output of ls directly into wc -l for counting.

steps.label: Filter with pipes
sentence: To search for 'root' in the output of ps aux: ps aux
grep 'root'

feedback.ok: Correct! ps aux | grep 'root' filters the process list to show only root processes.
feedback.bad: | passes ps output to grep for filtering.

steps.label: Pipe vs redirect
steps.question: What is the key difference between | (pipe) and > (redirect)?
options: | sends output to a file, > sends it to a command
| sends output to another command, > sends it to a file
| only works with grep, > works with all commands

feedback.ok: Correct! | connects commands to commands. > connects a command to a file.
feedback.bad: | sends output to another command. > saves output to a file. They're complementary tools.

steps.label: Reorder the pipeline
steps.instruction: Find how many .py files are in the current directory recursively:

feedback.ok: Correct! find produces the list, | pipes it to wc -l for counting.
feedback.bad: find . -name '*.py' | wc -l — find produces the list, then wc counts the lines.

steps.label: Write it yourself
steps.prompt: Find all lines containing 'warning' in app.log and count them.
steps.hint: Pipe grep into wc -l to count the matching lines.

output.bad:
<error>✗ Not quite.</error>
<info>Hint: grep 'warning' app.log | wc -l — grep filters, then wc counts.</info>

lessons.title: Section 3 review
lessons.description: Reinforce searching, finding, and piping with mixed exercises

steps.label: Review — grep flags
steps.question: Which grep flag shows only the COUNT of matching lines rather than the lines themselves?

feedback.ok: Correct! grep -c counts matches instead of printing them.
feedback.bad: grep -c counts the number of matching lines. -n shows line numbers, -v inverts, -i ignores case.

steps.label: Review — find by size
sentence: To find files larger than 500MB: find . -size

feedback.ok: Correct! + means 'greater than' in find -size. - means 'less than'.
feedback.bad: + means greater than. find . -size +500M finds files over 500MB.

steps.label: Review — pipe
sentence: To list only .txt files in /docs and count them: find /docs -name '*.txt'
wc -l

feedback.ok: Correct! Pipe the find output into wc -l for counting.
feedback.bad: | pipes the output of find into wc to count the results.

steps.label: Review — reorder
steps.instruction: Search case-insensitively for 'root' in /etc/passwd with line numbers:

feedback.ok: Correct! grep -in 'root' /etc/passwd
feedback.bad: grep [flags] [pattern] [file]: grep -in 'root' '/etc/passwd'

steps.label: Review — sort
steps.question: Why should you always sort a file before piping it to uniq?
options: uniq only removes consecutive duplicates — sort brings duplicates together
sort is required by uniq syntax

feedback.ok: Correct! uniq only removes lines that are next to each other. Sort first so all duplicates are adjacent.
feedback.bad: uniq only removes consecutive duplicates. If identical lines are scattered, sort must bring them together first.

steps.label: Review — write it
steps.prompt: Find all files modified in the last 7 days in the current directory.
steps.hint: Use find with -mtime and a negative number.

output.bad:
<error>✗ Not quite.</error>
<info>Hint: find . -mtime -7 finds files modified within the last 7 days.</info>

steps.label: Review — write it
steps.prompt: Show all running processes and filter to show only those containing 'node'.
steps.hint: Pipe ps aux into grep.

output.ok:
<success>✓ Correct! Pipeline working.</success>
<info>user  1234  0.5  2.1  node server.js
user  1235  0.1  1.8  node worker.js</info>
output.bad:
<error>✗ Not quite.</error>
<info>Hint: ps aux | grep node — pipe process list into grep to filter.</info>
